revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[sn:commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7677467]] Wikidata name: Takayama Festival [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}} [[Image:Kagura Tai Float.jpg|thumb|Takayama [[:en:Matsuri Float|Matsuri Float]]]] The {{Nihongo|'''Takayama Festivals'''|高山祭|Takayama Matsuri}} in [[:en:Takayama, Gifu|Takayama]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] started in the 16th to 17th century.<ref name="Lo, n.d.">Lo, P. (n.d.) Japan guide: Takayama and Gero onsen. Retrieved on August 06, 2009. From http://www.yamasa.org/japan/english/destinations/gifu/takayama.html</ref> The festivals are believed to have been started during the rule of the Kanamori family. Correspondence dated 1692 place the origin to 40 years prior to that date.<ref name="The Origin of, n.d.">Background of the Takayama matsuri or festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.hidanet.ne.jp/e02/ematsuri/ekigen.htm</ref> One of the festivals is held on 14 and 15 April and the other on 9 and 10 October.<ref name="Eder, 1972">Eder, M. (1972). "The folk customs museum in Takayama (Hida, Gifu prefecture)". ''Asian Folklore Studies'', 31(2), 148. Retrieved August 9, 2009 from Jstor Database.</ref>
The Spring Takayama Festival is centered on the Hie Shrine. The shrine is also known as the Sanno Shrine, and the spring festival is also known as the Sanno Festival. The Sanno Festival is held to pray for a good harvest and the Autumn Festival is for giving thanks.<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
The Autumn festival is centered on the Sakurayama [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] and is referred to as the Hachiman Festival. It is held after the crops are harvested. The fall festival is one of the three largest festivals in Japan. The other two are [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]'s [[Gion Matsuri]] and the [[:en:Chichibu Night Festival|Chichibu Night Festival]] Matsuri.<ref name="The other two, n.d.">The Takayama matsuri (festival). Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref>
== Floats == The festivals are famous for the large ornate floats, or ''[[yatai (float)|yatai]]'', which roam around the city at night. The floats date back to the 17th century, and are decorated with intricate carvings of gilded wood and detailed metal-work, similar in style to art from Kyoto during the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]], and blended with elements from the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Detailed carving, lacquering and beautiful decorative metal-works is found not only on the outside of the floats, but inside as well, under the roof and behind the panels, where the work is amazingly detailed.<ref name="The Yatai date back, n.d.">The festive floats. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The floats are also “gorgeously decorated with embroidered drapery."<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
"The ''yatai'' floats are lined up before dusk, and once the town becomes veiled in the evening darkness, as many as 100 [[Traditional lighting equipment of Japan#Chōchin|''chochin'' lanterns]] are lit on each of the floats. The unique ornaments of the ''yatai'' floats look even more resplendent in the darkness of the night".<ref name="The Yatai Floats are, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Autumn Festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/traditionalevents/a54_fes_takayama.html</ref> The floats are moved around the city by people but are wheeled carts and the bearers are not required to endure the load. The floats are lit by traditional lanterns and escorted on a tour of the city by people in traditional kimono or ''hakama''. Each float reflects the district in Takayama to which it represents. The craftsmanship and the Hotei tai "has intricate marionettes" which perform on top.<ref name="Hotei tai">The puppets. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The puppet show is registered as a "cultural asset”.<ref name="Eder, 1972" /> The tall festive floats are displayed during the two days of both festivals. During inclement weather the floats are returned to their storage houses. The Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan stores four of the 11 fall floats; the others are stored in special storehouses throughout the city, when not in use. During inclement weather, the outer doors to the Yatai Kaikan are open so visitors may view them. The floats in the Yatai Kaikan are changed several times a year.
The Yatai Kaikan is located in the northern end of Takayama's old town, a 15–20 minute walk from the station. The Yatai Kaikan is open is from 08:30 to 17:00 from March to November and from 09:00 to 16:30 from December to February.<ref name="The Takayama Matsuri Yatai, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.
== Marionettes == The marionettes are made of wood, silk and brocade or embroidered cloth. They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on [[:en:Budai|Hotei]] Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. The springs in the puppets are made of [[:en:Right whale|Right whale]] [[:en:Baleen|Baleen]] and cannot be replaced with steel springs or the baleen of other whales. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />
== See also ==
* [[:en:Takayama, Gifu|Takayama, Gifu]]

Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.
== Marionettes == The marionettes are made of wood, silk and brocade or embroidered cloth. They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on [[:en:Budai|Hotei]] Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. The springs in the puppets are made of [[:en:Right whale|Right whale]] [[:en:Baleen|Baleen]] and cannot be replaced with steel springs or the baleen of other whales. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />

[[en:Takayama Festival]]
<!--Takayama Festival--><!--Takayama Festival--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]] {{Commons category|Takayama Festival}} [[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]] [[gur:Takayama Festivals]] [[it:Takayama Matsuri]] [[ja:高山祭]] [[mos:Takayama Festival]] [[zh:高山祭]] {{wikidata link|Q7677467}} [[Category:Culture in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Takayama, Gifu]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[sn:commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7677467]] Wikidata name: Takayama Festival [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}} [[Image:Kagura Tai Float.jpg|thumb|Takayama [[:en:Matsuri Float|Matsuri Float]]]] The {{Nihongo|'''Takayama Festivals'''|高山祭|Takayama Matsuri}} in [[:en:Takayama, Gifu|Takayama]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] started in the 16th to 17th century.<ref name="Lo, n.d.">Lo, P. (n.d.) Japan guide: Takayama and Gero onsen. Retrieved on August 06, 2009. From http://www.yamasa.org/japan/english/destinations/gifu/takayama.html</ref> The festivals are believed to have been started during the rule of the Kanamori family. Correspondence dated 1692 place the origin to 40 years prior to that date.<ref name="The Origin of, n.d.">Background of the Takayama matsuri or festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.hidanet.ne.jp/e02/ematsuri/ekigen.htm</ref> One of the festivals is held on 14 and 15 April and the other on 9 and 10 October.<ref name="Eder, 1972">Eder, M. (1972). "The folk customs museum in Takayama (Hida, Gifu prefecture)". ''Asian Folklore Studies'', 31(2), 148. Retrieved August 9, 2009 from Jstor Database.</ref>
The Spring Takayama Festival is centered on the Hie Shrine. The shrine is also known as the Sanno Shrine, and the spring festival is also known as the Sanno Festival. The Sanno Festival is held to pray for a good harvest and the Autumn Festival is for giving thanks.<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
The Autumn festival is centered on the Sakurayama [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] and is referred to as the Hachiman Festival. It is held after the crops are harvested. The fall festival is one of the three largest festivals in Japan. The other two are [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]'s [[Gion Matsuri]] and the [[:en:Chichibu Night Festival|Chichibu Night Festival]] Matsuri.<ref name="The other two, n.d.">The Takayama matsuri (festival). Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref>
== Floats == The festivals are famous for the large ornate floats, or ''[[yatai (float)|yatai]]'', which roam around the city at night. The floats date back to the 17th century, and are decorated with intricate carvings of gilded wood and detailed metal-work, similar in style to art from Kyoto during the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]], and blended with elements from the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Detailed carving, lacquering and beautiful decorative metal-works is found not only on the outside of the floats, but inside as well, under the roof and behind the panels, where the work is amazingly detailed.<ref name="The Yatai date back, n.d.">The festive floats. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The floats are also “gorgeously decorated with embroidered drapery."<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
"The ''yatai'' floats are lined up before dusk, and once the town becomes veiled in the evening darkness, as many as 100 [[Traditional lighting equipment of Japan#Chōchin|''chochin'' lanterns]] are lit on each of the floats. The unique ornaments of the ''yatai'' floats look even more resplendent in the darkness of the night".<ref name="The Yatai Floats are, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Autumn Festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/traditionalevents/a54_fes_takayama.html</ref> The floats are moved around the city by people but are wheeled carts and the bearers are not required to endure the load. The floats are lit by traditional lanterns and escorted on a tour of the city by people in traditional kimono or ''hakama''. Each float reflects the district in Takayama to which it represents. The craftsmanship and the Hotei tai "has intricate marionettes" which perform on top.<ref name="Hotei tai">The puppets. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The puppet show is registered as a "cultural asset”.<ref name="Eder, 1972" /> The tall festive floats are displayed during the two days of both festivals. During inclement weather the floats are returned to their storage houses. The Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan stores four of the 11 fall floats; the others are stored in special storehouses throughout the city, when not in use. During inclement weather, the outer doors to the Yatai Kaikan are open so visitors may view them. The floats in the Yatai Kaikan are changed several times a year.
The Yatai Kaikan is located in the northern end of Takayama's old town, a 15–20 minute walk from the station. The Yatai Kaikan is open is from 08:30 to 17:00 from March to November and from 09:00 to 16:30 from December to February.<ref name="The Takayama Matsuri Yatai, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.
== Marionettes == The marionettes are made of wood, silk and brocade or embroidered cloth. They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on [[:en:Budai|Hotei]] Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. The springs in the puppets are made of [[:en:Right whale|Right whale]] [[:en:Baleen|Baleen]] and cannot be replaced with steel springs or the baleen of other whales. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />

The floats date back to the 17th century, and are decorated with intricate carvings of gilded wood and detailed metal-work, similar in style to art from Kyoto during the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, and blended with elements from the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Detailed carving, lacquering and beautiful decorative metal-works is found not only on the outside of the floats, but inside as well, under the roof and behind the panels, where the work is amazingly detailed.<ref name="The Yatai date back, n.d.">The festive floats. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The floats are also “gorgeously decorated with embroidered drapery."<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
"The ''yatai'' floats are lined up before dusk, and once the town becomes veiled in the evening darkness, as many as 100 [[Traditional lighting equipment of Japan#Chōchin|''chochin'' lanterns]] are lit on each of the floats. The unique ornaments of the ''yatai'' floats look even more resplendent in the darkness of the night".<ref name="The Yatai Floats are, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Autumn Festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/traditionalevents/a54_fes_takayama.html</ref> The floats are moved around the city by people but are wheeled carts and the bearers are not required to endure the load. The floats are lit by traditional lanterns and escorted on a tour of the city by people in traditional kimono or ''hakama''. Each float reflects the district in Takayama to which it represents. The craftsmanship and the Hotei tai "has intricate marionettes" which perform on top.<ref name="Hotei tai">The puppets. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The puppet show is registered as a "cultural asset”.<ref name="Eder, 1972" /> The tall festive floats are displayed during the two days of both festivals. During inclement weather the floats are returned to their storage houses. The Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan stores four of the 11 fall floats; the others are stored in special storehouses throughout the city, when not in use. During inclement weather, the outer doors to the Yatai Kaikan are open so visitors may view them. The floats in the Yatai Kaikan are changed several times a year.
The Yatai Kaikan is located in the northern end of Takayama's old town, a 15–20 minute walk from the station. The Yatai Kaikan is open is from 08:30 to 17:00 from March to November and from 09:00 to 16:30 from December to February.<ref name="The Takayama Matsuri Yatai, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.
== Marionettes == The marionettes are made of wood, silk and brocade or embroidered cloth. They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei}} Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. The springs in the puppets are made of {{ill|Right whale|en|Right whale|WD=Q1527843|lt=Right whale}} {{ill|Baleen|en|Baleen|WD=Q275426|lt=Baleen}} and cannot be replaced with steel springs or the baleen of other whales. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Takayama, Gifu|en|Takayama, Gifu|WD=Q622594|lt=Takayama, Gifu}}

(n.d.) Japan guide: Takayama and Gero onsen. Retrieved on August 06, 2009. From http://www.yamasa.org/japan/english/destinations/gifu/takayama.html</ref> The festivals are believed to have been started during the rule of the Kanamori family. Correspondence dated 1692 place the origin to 40 years prior to that date.<ref name="The Origin of, n.d.">Background of the Takayama matsuri or festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.hidanet.ne.jp/e02/ematsuri/ekigen.htm</ref> One of the festivals is held on 14 and 15 April and the other on 9 and 10 October.<ref name="Eder, 1972">Eder, M. (1972). "The folk customs museum in Takayama (Hida, Gifu prefecture)". ''Asian Folklore Studies'', 31(2), 148. Retrieved August 9, 2009 from Jstor Database.</ref>
The Spring Takayama Festival is centered on the Hie Shrine. The shrine is also known as the Sanno Shrine, and the spring festival is also known as the Sanno Festival. The Sanno Festival is held to pray for a good harvest and the Autumn Festival is for giving thanks.<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
The Autumn festival is centered on the Sakurayama [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] and is referred to as the Hachiman Festival. It is held after the crops are harvested. The fall festival is one of the three largest festivals in Japan. The other two are {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}'s [[Gion Matsuri]] and the {{ill|Chichibu Night Festival|en|Chichibu Night Festival|WD=Q11596116|lt=Chichibu Night Festival}} Matsuri.<ref name="The other two, n.d.">The Takayama matsuri (festival). Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref>
== Floats == The festivals are famous for the large ornate floats, or ''[[yatai (float)|yatai]]'', which roam around the city at night. The floats date back to the 17th century, and are decorated with intricate carvings of gilded wood and detailed metal-work, similar in style to art from Kyoto during the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, and blended with elements from the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Detailed carving, lacquering and beautiful decorative metal-works is found not only on the outside of the floats, but inside as well, under the roof and behind the panels, where the work is amazingly detailed.<ref name="The Yatai date back, n.d.">The festive floats. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The floats are also “gorgeously decorated with embroidered drapery."<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
"The ''yatai'' floats are lined up before dusk, and once the town becomes veiled in the evening darkness, as many as 100 [[Traditional lighting equipment of Japan#Chōchin|''chochin'' lanterns]] are lit on each of the floats. The unique ornaments of the ''yatai'' floats look even more resplendent in the darkness of the night".<ref name="The Yatai Floats are, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Autumn Festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/traditionalevents/a54_fes_takayama.html</ref> The floats are moved around the city by people but are wheeled carts and the bearers are not required to endure the load. The floats are lit by traditional lanterns and escorted on a tour of the city by people in traditional kimono or ''hakama''. Each float reflects the district in Takayama to which it represents. The craftsmanship and the Hotei tai "has intricate marionettes" which perform on top.<ref name="Hotei tai">The puppets. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The puppet show is registered as a "cultural asset”.<ref name="Eder, 1972" /> The tall festive floats are displayed during the two days of both festivals. During inclement weather the floats are returned to their storage houses. The Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan stores four of the 11 fall floats; the others are stored in special storehouses throughout the city, when not in use. During inclement weather, the outer doors to the Yatai Kaikan are open so visitors may view them. The floats in the Yatai Kaikan are changed several times a year.
The Yatai Kaikan is located in the northern end of Takayama's old town, a 15–20 minute walk from the station. The Yatai Kaikan is open is from 08:30 to 17:00 from March to November and from 09:00 to 16:30 from December to February.<ref name="The Takayama Matsuri Yatai, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.
== Marionettes == The marionettes are made of wood, silk and brocade or embroidered cloth. They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei}} Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. The springs in the puppets are made of {{ill|Right whale|en|Right whale|WD=Q1527843|lt=Right whale}} {{ill|Baleen|en|Baleen|WD=Q275426|lt=Baleen}} and cannot be replaced with steel springs or the baleen of other whales. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[co:commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[co:commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[sn:commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7677467]] Wikidata name: Takayama Festival [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}} [[Image:Kagura Tai Float.jpg|thumb|Takayama [[:en:Matsuri Float|Matsuri Float]]]] The {{Nihongo|'''Takayama Festivals'''|高山祭|Takayama Matsuri}} in {{ill|Takayama, Gifu|en|Takayama, Gifu|WD=Q622594|lt=Takayama}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} started in the 16th to 17th century.<ref name="Lo, n.d.">Lo, P. (n.d.) Japan guide: Takayama and Gero onsen. Retrieved on August 06, 2009. From http://www.yamasa.org/japan/english/destinations/gifu/takayama.html</ref> The festivals are believed to have been started during the rule of the Kanamori family. Correspondence dated 1692 place the origin to 40 years prior to that date.<ref name="The Origin of, n.d.">Background of the Takayama matsuri or festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.hidanet.ne.jp/e02/ematsuri/ekigen.htm</ref> One of the festivals is held on 14 and 15 April and the other on 9 and 10 October.<ref name="Eder, 1972">Eder, M. (1972). "The folk customs museum in Takayama (Hida, Gifu prefecture)". ''Asian Folklore Studies'', 31(2), 148. Retrieved August 9, 2009 from Jstor Database.</ref>
The Spring Takayama Festival is centered on the Hie Shrine. The shrine is also known as the Sanno Shrine, and the spring festival is also known as the Sanno Festival. The Sanno Festival is held to pray for a good harvest and the Autumn Festival is for giving thanks.<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
The Autumn festival is centered on the Sakurayama [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] and is referred to as the Hachiman Festival. It is held after the crops are harvested. The fall festival is one of the three largest festivals in Japan. The other two are {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}'s [[Gion Matsuri]] and the {{ill|Chichibu Night Festival|en|Chichibu Night Festival|WD=Q11596116|lt=Chichibu Night Festival}} Matsuri.<ref name="The other two, n.d.">The Takayama matsuri (festival). Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref>
== Floats == The festivals are famous for the large ornate floats, or ''[[yatai (float)|yatai]]'', which roam around the city at night. The floats date back to the 17th century, and are decorated with intricate carvings of gilded wood and detailed metal-work, similar in style to art from Kyoto during the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, and blended with elements from the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Detailed carving, lacquering and beautiful decorative metal-works is found not only on the outside of the floats, but inside as well, under the roof and behind the panels, where the work is amazingly detailed.<ref name="The Yatai date back, n.d.">The festive floats. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The floats are also “gorgeously decorated with embroidered drapery."<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
"The ''yatai'' floats are lined up before dusk, and once the town becomes veiled in the evening darkness, as many as 100 [[Traditional lighting equipment of Japan#Chōchin|''chochin'' lanterns]] are lit on each of the floats. The unique ornaments of the ''yatai'' floats look even more resplendent in the darkness of the night".<ref name="The Yatai Floats are, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Autumn Festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/traditionalevents/a54_fes_takayama.html</ref> The floats are moved around the city by people but are wheeled carts and the bearers are not required to endure the load. The floats are lit by traditional lanterns and escorted on a tour of the city by people in traditional kimono or ''hakama''. Each float reflects the district in Takayama to which it represents. The craftsmanship and the Hotei tai "has intricate marionettes" which perform on top.<ref name="Hotei tai">The puppets. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The puppet show is registered as a "cultural asset”.<ref name="Eder, 1972" /> The tall festive floats are displayed during the two days of both festivals. During inclement weather the floats are returned to their storage houses. The Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan stores four of the 11 fall floats; the others are stored in special storehouses throughout the city, when not in use. During inclement weather, the outer doors to the Yatai Kaikan are open so visitors may view them. The floats in the Yatai Kaikan are changed several times a year.
The Yatai Kaikan is located in the northern end of Takayama's old town, a 15–20 minute walk from the station. The Yatai Kaikan is open is from 08:30 to 17:00 from March to November and from 09:00 to 16:30 from December to February.<ref name="The Takayama Matsuri Yatai, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.
== Marionettes == The marionettes are made of wood, silk and brocade or embroidered cloth. They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei}} Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. The springs in the puppets are made of {{ill|Right whale|en|Right whale|WD=Q1527843|lt=Right whale}} {{ill|Baleen|en|Baleen|WD=Q275426|lt=Baleen}} and cannot be replaced with steel springs or the baleen of other whales. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />

The Yatai Kaikan is located in the northern end of Takayama's old town, a 15–20 minute walk from the station. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.
== Marionettes == The marionettes are made of wood, silk and brocade or embroidered cloth. They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei}} Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. The springs in the puppets are made of {{ill|Right whale|en|Right whale|WD=Q1527843|lt=Right whale}} {{ill|Baleen|en|Baleen|WD=Q275426|lt=Baleen}} and cannot be replaced with steel springs or the baleen of other whales. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Takayama, Gifu|en|Takayama, Gifu|WD=Q622594|lt=Takayama, Gifu}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[co:commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[co:commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[sn:commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]]
{{Short description|Japanese festival}} [[Image:Kagura Tai Float.jpg|thumb|Takayama [[:en:Matsuri Float|Matsuri Float]]]] The {{Nihongo|'''Takayama Festivals'''|高山祭|Takayama Matsuri}} in {{ill|Takayama, Gifu|en|Takayama, Gifu|WD=Q622594|lt=Takayama}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} started in the 16th to 17th century.<ref name="Lo, n.d.">Lo, P. (n.d.) Japan guide: Takayama and Gero onsen. Retrieved on August 06, 2009. From http://www.yamasa.org/japan/english/destinations/gifu/takayama.html</ref> The festivals are believed to have been started during the rule of the Kanamori family. Correspondence dated 1692 place the origin to 40 years prior to that date.<ref name="The Origin of, n.d.">Background of the Takayama matsuri or festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.hidanet.ne.jp/e02/ematsuri/ekigen.htm</ref> One of the festivals is held on 14 and 15 April and the other on 9 and 10 October.<ref name="Eder, 1972">Eder, M. (1972). "The folk customs museum in Takayama (Hida, Gifu prefecture)". ''Asian Folklore Studies'', 31(2), 148. Retrieved August 9, 2009 from Jstor Database.</ref>
The Spring Takayama Festival is centered on the Hie Shrine. The shrine is also known as the Sanno Shrine, and the spring festival is also known as the Sanno Festival. The Sanno Festival is held to pray for a good harvest and the Autumn Festival is for giving thanks.<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
The Autumn festival is centered on the Sakurayama [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] and is referred to as the Hachiman Festival. It is held after the crops are harvested. The fall festival is one of the three largest festivals in Japan. The other two are {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}'s [[Gion Matsuri]] and the {{ill|Chichibu Night Festival|en|Chichibu Night Festival|WD=Q11596116|lt=Chichibu Night Festival}} Matsuri.<ref name="The other two, n.d.">The Takayama matsuri (festival). Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref>
== Floats == The festivals are famous for the large ornate floats, or ''[[yatai (float)|yatai]]'', which roam around the city at night. The floats date back to the 17th century, and are decorated with intricate carvings of gilded wood and detailed metal-work, similar in style to art from Kyoto during the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, and blended with elements from the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Detailed carving, lacquering and beautiful decorative metal-works is found not only on the outside of the floats, but inside as well, under the roof and behind the panels, where the work is amazingly detailed.<ref name="The Yatai date back, n.d.">The festive floats. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The floats are also “gorgeously decorated with embroidered drapery."<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
"The ''yatai'' floats are lined up before dusk, and once the town becomes veiled in the evening darkness, as many as 100 [[Traditional lighting equipment of Japan#Chōchin|''chochin'' lanterns]] are lit on each of the floats. The unique ornaments of the ''yatai'' floats look even more resplendent in the darkness of the night".<ref name="The Yatai Floats are, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Autumn Festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/traditionalevents/a54_fes_takayama.html</ref> The floats are moved around the city by people but are wheeled carts and the bearers are not required to endure the load. The floats are lit by traditional lanterns and escorted on a tour of the city by people in traditional kimono or ''hakama''. Each float reflects the district in Takayama to which it represents. The craftsmanship and the Hotei tai "has intricate marionettes" which perform on top.<ref name="Hotei tai">The puppets. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The puppet show is registered as a "cultural asset”.<ref name="Eder, 1972" /> The tall festive floats are displayed during the two days of both festivals. During inclement weather the floats are returned to their storage houses. The Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan stores four of the 11 fall floats; the others are stored in special storehouses throughout the city, when not in use. During inclement weather, the outer doors to the Yatai Kaikan are open so visitors may view them. The floats in the Yatai Kaikan are changed several times a year.
The Yatai Kaikan is located in the northern end of Takayama's old town, a 15–20 minute walk from the station. The Yatai Kaikan is open is from 08:30 to 17:00 from March to November and from 09:00 to 16:30 from December to February.<ref name="The Takayama Matsuri Yatai, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.
== Marionettes == The marionettes are made of wood, silk and brocade or embroidered cloth. They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei}} Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. The springs in the puppets are made of {{ill|Right whale|en|Right whale|WD=Q1527843|lt=Right whale}} {{ill|Baleen|en|Baleen|WD=Q275426|lt=Baleen}} and cannot be replaced with steel springs or the baleen of other whales. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Takayama, Gifu|en|Takayama, Gifu|WD=Q622594|lt=Takayama, Gifu}}

Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref>
== Floats == The festivals are famous for the large ornate floats, or ''[[yatai (float)|yatai]]'', which roam around the city at night. The floats date back to the 17th century, and are decorated with intricate carvings of gilded wood and detailed metal-work, similar in style to art from Kyoto during the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, and blended with elements from the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Detailed carving, lacquering and beautiful decorative metal-works is found not only on the outside of the floats, but inside as well, under the roof and behind the panels, where the work is amazingly detailed.<ref name="The Yatai date back, n.d.">The festive floats. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The floats are also “gorgeously decorated with embroidered drapery."<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
"The ''yatai'' floats are lined up before dusk, and once the town becomes veiled in the evening darkness, as many as 100 [[Traditional lighting equipment of Japan#Chōchin|''chochin'' lanterns]] are lit on each of the floats. The unique ornaments of the ''yatai'' floats look even more resplendent in the darkness of the night".<ref name="The Yatai Floats are, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Autumn Festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/traditionalevents/a54_fes_takayama.html</ref> The floats are moved around the city by people but are wheeled carts and the bearers are not required to endure the load. The floats are lit by traditional lanterns and escorted on a tour of the city by people in traditional kimono or ''hakama''. Each float reflects the district in Takayama to which it represents. The craftsmanship and the Hotei tai "has intricate marionettes" which perform on top.<ref name="Hotei tai">The puppets. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The puppet show is registered as a "cultural asset”.<ref name="Eder, 1972" /> The tall festive floats are displayed during the two days of both festivals. During inclement weather the floats are returned to their storage houses. The Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan stores four of the 11 fall floats; the others are stored in special storehouses throughout the city, when not in use. During inclement weather, the outer doors to the Yatai Kaikan are open so visitors may view them. The floats in the Yatai Kaikan are changed several times a year.
The Yatai Kaikan is located in the northern end of Takayama's old town, a 15–20 minute walk from the station. The Yatai Kaikan is open is from 08:30 to 17:00 from March to November and from 09:00 to 16:30 from December to February.<ref name="The Takayama Matsuri Yatai, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.
== Marionettes == The marionettes are made of wood, silk and brocade or embroidered cloth. They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei}} Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. The springs in the puppets are made of {{ill|Right whale|en|Right whale|WD=Q1527843|lt=Right whale}} {{ill|Baleen|en|Baleen|WD=Q275426|lt=Baleen}} and cannot be replaced with steel springs or the baleen of other whales. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Takayama, Gifu|en|Takayama, Gifu|WD=Q622594|lt=Takayama, Gifu}}

(n.d.) Japan guide: Takayama and Gero onsen. Retrieved on August 06, 2009. From http://www.yamasa.org/japan/english/destinations/gifu/takayama.html</ref> The festivals are believed to have been started during the rule of the Kanamori family. Correspondence dated 1692 place the origin to 40 years prior to that date.<ref name="The Origin of, n.d.">Background of the Takayama matsuri or festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.hidanet.ne.jp/e02/ematsuri/ekigen.htm</ref> One of the festivals is held on 14 and 15 April and the other on 9 and 10 October.<ref name="Eder, 1972">Eder, M. (1972). "The folk customs museum in Takayama (Hida, Gifu prefecture)". ''Asian Folklore Studies'', 31(2), 148. Retrieved August 9, 2009 from Jstor Database.</ref>
The Spring Takayama Festival is centered on the Hie Shrine. The shrine is also known as the Sanno Shrine, and the spring festival is also known as the Sanno Festival. The Sanno Festival is held to pray for a good harvest and the Autumn Festival is for giving thanks.<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
The Autumn festival is centered on the Sakurayama [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] and is referred to as the Hachiman Festival. It is held after the crops are harvested. The fall festival is one of the three largest festivals in Japan. The other two are {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}'s [[Gion Matsuri]] and the {{ill|Chichibu Night Festival|en|Chichibu Night Festival|WD=Q11596116|lt=Chichibu Night Festival|1=Chichibu Night Festival}} Matsuri.<ref name="The other two, n.d.">The Takayama matsuri (festival). Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref>

Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The floats are also “gorgeously decorated with embroidered drapery."<ref name="Eder, 1972" />

Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The puppet show is registered as a "cultural asset”.<ref name="Eder, 1972" /> The tall festive floats are displayed during the two days of both festivals. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.

They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei|1=Budai}} Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[co:commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[co:commons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مهرجانات تاكاياما]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Takayama Festival]][[en:Takayama Festival]][[fr:Festivals Takayama de Gifu]][[gur:Takayama Festivals]][[it:Takayama Matsuri]][[ja:高山祭]][[mos:Takayama Festival]][[zh:高山祭]]
{{Short description|Japanese festival}} [[Image:Kagura Tai Float.jpg|thumb|Takayama [[:en:Matsuri Float|Matsuri Float]]]] The {{Nihongo|'''Takayama Festivals'''|高山祭|Takayama Matsuri}} in {{ill|Takayama, Gifu|en|Takayama, Gifu|WD=Q622594|lt=Takayama|1=Takayama, Gifu}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} started in the 16th to 17th century.<ref name="Lo, n.d.">Lo, P. (n.d.) Japan guide: Takayama and Gero onsen. Retrieved on August 06, 2009. From http://www.yamasa.org/japan/english/destinations/gifu/takayama.html</ref> The festivals are believed to have been started during the rule of the Kanamori family. Correspondence dated 1692 place the origin to 40 years prior to that date.<ref name="The Origin of, n.d.">Background of the Takayama matsuri or festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.hidanet.ne.jp/e02/ematsuri/ekigen.htm</ref> One of the festivals is held on 14 and 15 April and the other on 9 and 10 October.<ref name="Eder, 1972">Eder, M. (1972). "The folk customs museum in Takayama (Hida, Gifu prefecture)". ''Asian Folklore Studies'', 31(2), 148. Retrieved August 9, 2009 from Jstor Database.</ref>
The Spring Takayama Festival is centered on the Hie Shrine. The shrine is also known as the Sanno Shrine, and the spring festival is also known as the Sanno Festival. The Sanno Festival is held to pray for a good harvest and the Autumn Festival is for giving thanks.<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
The Autumn festival is centered on the Sakurayama [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] and is referred to as the Hachiman Festival. It is held after the crops are harvested. The fall festival is one of the three largest festivals in Japan. The other two are {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}'s [[Gion Matsuri]] and the {{ill|Chichibu Night Festival|en|Chichibu Night Festival|WD=Q11596116|lt=Chichibu Night Festival|1=Chichibu Night Festival}} Matsuri.<ref name="The other two, n.d.">The Takayama matsuri (festival). Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref>
== Floats == The festivals are famous for the large ornate floats, or ''[[yatai (float)|yatai]]'', which roam around the city at night. The floats date back to the 17th century, and are decorated with intricate carvings of gilded wood and detailed metal-work, similar in style to art from Kyoto during the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, and blended with elements from the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Detailed carving, lacquering and beautiful decorative metal-works is found not only on the outside of the floats, but inside as well, under the roof and behind the panels, where the work is amazingly detailed.<ref name="The Yatai date back, n.d.">The festive floats. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The floats are also “gorgeously decorated with embroidered drapery."<ref name="Eder, 1972" />
"The ''yatai'' floats are lined up before dusk, and once the town becomes veiled in the evening darkness, as many as 100 [[Traditional lighting equipment of Japan#Chōchin|''chochin'' lanterns]] are lit on each of the floats. The unique ornaments of the ''yatai'' floats look even more resplendent in the darkness of the night".<ref name="The Yatai Floats are, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Autumn Festival. Retrieved August 09, 2009. from Japan National Tourism Organization web site: http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/traditionalevents/a54_fes_takayama.html</ref> The floats are moved around the city by people but are wheeled carts and the bearers are not required to endure the load. The floats are lit by traditional lanterns and escorted on a tour of the city by people in traditional kimono or ''hakama''. Each float reflects the district in Takayama to which it represents. The craftsmanship and the Hotei tai "has intricate marionettes" which perform on top.<ref name="Hotei tai">The puppets. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/02_festival/index.html</ref> The puppet show is registered as a "cultural asset”.<ref name="Eder, 1972" /> The tall festive floats are displayed during the two days of both festivals. During inclement weather the floats are returned to their storage houses. The Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan stores four of the 11 fall floats; the others are stored in special storehouses throughout the city, when not in use. During inclement weather, the outer doors to the Yatai Kaikan are open so visitors may view them. The floats in the Yatai Kaikan are changed several times a year.
The Yatai Kaikan is located in the northern end of Takayama's old town, a 15–20 minute walk from the station. The Yatai Kaikan is open is from 08:30 to 17:00 from March to November and from 09:00 to 16:30 from December to February.<ref name="The Takayama Matsuri Yatai, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.
== Marionettes == The marionettes are made of wood, silk and brocade or embroidered cloth. They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei|1=Budai}} Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. The springs in the puppets are made of {{ill|Right whale|en|Right whale|WD=Q1527843|lt=Right whale|1=Right whale}} {{ill|Baleen|en|Baleen|WD=Q275426|lt=Baleen|1=Baleen}} and cannot be replaced with steel springs or the baleen of other whales. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Takayama, Gifu|en|Takayama, Gifu|WD=Q622594|lt=Takayama, Gifu|1=Takayama, Gifu}}
== References ==

{{coord missing|Gifu Prefecture}}
==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]]* [http://cgi2.nhk.or.jp/michi/cgi/detail.cgi?dasID=D0004200010_00000 Takayama Matsuri Festival]{{ill|NHK|en|NHK|WD=Q212128|lt=NHK|1=NHK}}(video) * [http://www.hida.jp/english/ Takayama official website] {{in lang|en}} * [http://www.takayama-guide.com Takayama Guide] {{in lang|en}} * [http://sightseeing.in-japan.jp/2007/05/stepping_back_in_time_in_takay.html Stepping back in time in Takayama] {{in lang|en}} * {{Wikivoyage-inline|Hida-Takayama}} {{in lang|en}} * [http://travel.kankou-gifu.jp/en/see-and-do/2/ Gifu travel guide] {{in lang|en}} * [http://gifucrossroads.wordpress.com/ Gifu crossroads (blog about tourism in Gifu)] {{in lang|en}}

The Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan stores four of the 11 fall floats; the others are stored in special storehouses throughout the city, when not in use. During inclement weather, the outer doors to the Yatai Kaikan are open so visitors may view them. The floats in the Yatai Kaikan are changed several times a year.
The Yatai Kaikan is located in the northern end of Takayama's old town, a 15–20 minute walk from the station. The Yatai Kaikan is open is from 08:30 to 17:00 from March to November and from 09:00 to 16:30 from December to February.<ref name="The Takayama Matsuri Yatai, n.d.">Takayama Matsuri Yatai Kaikan. Retrieved August 07, 2009. from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/05_yataikaikan/index.html</ref> The admission fee is 840 yen.
== Marionettes == The marionettes are made of wood, silk and brocade or embroidered cloth. They are operated by strings and push rods from within the yatai.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d.">Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Hida-Takayama Sakurayama Hachimangu Shrine Web site: http://www.hida-hachiman.org/en/04_puppets/index.html</ref> “Karakuri (mechanical) puppet plays performed on a stage are superb”.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d.">Nakabayashi, T. (n.d.) The Takayama festival and whale baleen. Retrieved August 8, 2009, from Japan Whaling Association Web site: http://www.whaling.jp/english/isana/no32_01.html</ref> The puppets like the Yatai represent the skilled craftsmen of the area. The three marionettes “on {{ill|Budai|en|Budai|WD=Q599838|lt=Hotei|1=Budai}} Tai (the god of fortune)” require 9 puppet masters to manipulate the 36 strings which make the marionettes move in a lifelike manner, with gestures, turns and other movements.<ref name="The puppets or marionettes, n.d."/> A problem with the puppets are parts needed to repair the puppets. The springs in the puppets are made of {{ill|Right whale|en|Right whale|WD=Q1527843|lt=Right whale|1=Right whale}} {{ill|Baleen|en|Baleen|WD=Q275426|lt=Baleen|1=Baleen}} and cannot be replaced with steel springs or the baleen of other whales. Other materials used to make the springs cannot duplicate the movements of the springs made with Right whale baleen.<ref name="Nakabayashi, n.d." />
